entry.title: Re: Switching from domain knowledge to consultant role
entry.content:
Your background is a strong foundation for moving into an SAP functional consultant role. You already have the domain expertise in SCM, hands‑on SAP MM knowledge, and experience supporting implementation and UATs. The transition is about reframing your profile and gaining structured exposure to the consultant’s way of working.

How the transition works

Domain expertise first: Your 12 years in SCM front‑end (RFP/RFQ, negotiations, PO handling, vendor management) are your biggest asset. Clients want consultants who understand procurement realities.

Functional consultant role: You act as the bridge between business and system. You gather requirements, design processes, validate solutions, and support testing. Configuration is part of the toolkit, but not the only skill.

Configuration knowledge: While you won’t be expected to configure everything from day one, you should understand the basics (release strategies, pricing, account determination, purchasing document types). This helps you speak the same language as technical consultants and know what’s feasible.

Recommended approach

Strengthen process + system mapping
Learn how business requirements translate into SAP functionality. Practice writing functional specifications (FS) for developers.

Get exposure to configuration
You don’t need to master every IMG node, but knowing the key customizing areas in MM will make you credible. Think of configuration knowledge as “awareness + ability to test,” not “daily job.”

Focus on consulting skills
Requirement gathering, fit‑gap analysis, UAT preparation, issue resolution. Documentation and communication with both business and technical teams.

Quality landscape experience
As a consultant, you’ll test configuration in quality, validate end‑to‑end scenarios, and guide users. You’ll be the one ensuring that what’s configured matches business needs before it moves to production.

Next step

Your best move is to combine your SCM domain expertise with structured SAP MM functional knowledge. That means:

  • Learn configuration basics (not to become a configurator, but to understand the system’s levers).
  • Build consulting skills (requirements, FS, UAT, issue resolution).
  • Position yourself as a domain expert who can now functionally consult on SAP MM.

entry.title: Re: Who and how to create or configure HSN code
entry.content:
Hi @BANDLAVENKATESH ,
Normally HSN Codes is created by Functional Consultant (FI/MM/SD). Typically responsible for configuring HSN codes in SAP, since it impacts tax determination and material master data. Business/Tax Team: Provides the correct HSN code and slab details (GST % rates, exemptions, etc.).
Basis/Technical Team: Not directly involved, unless enhancements or custom tables are required.

Please find below the step‑by‑step process to create HSN in SAP
1. Create HSN Code in SAP
Use Transaction Code: J1ID
Navigate to Chapter IDs → Create new entry
Enter:HSN Code (numeric code provided by tax team)
Description (material category)
Tax Rates/Slabs (CGST, SGST, IGST percentages)

2. Assign HSN Code to Material
Use Transaction Code: MM02 (Change Material Master)
Go to Sales: Sales Org. Data 1 or Foreign Trade: Export Data tab (depending on version)
Enter the HSN Code in the relevant field
Save changes

3. Maintain Condition Records
Use Transaction Code: VK11
Select the relevant Condition Type (e.g., GST condition types like JGST, JCGST, JSGST, JIGST)
Enter: HSN Code
Tax Rate/Slab
Validity period
Save condition record

4. Testing
Create a Sales Order/Invoice
System should automatically pick the correct tax rate based on the HSN code assigned to the material.

You must always confirm the correct HSN code with the tax compliance team before configuration.

Hope I answer your question.

entry.title: Re: Lean Service Enablement for Project Networks
entry.content:
Hi @Mario_Franz,

First of all, I would like to express my appreciation for this comprehensive documentation.                                                                                                                           

We are currently encountering an issue when generating Purchase Requisitions from components in SAP Project System for Lean Services. The scenario concerns materials of type SRV with Product Type Group 2 (Lean Services), within a project and network configured in Enhanced Procurement Mode. This mode has been correctly activated at both the Project Profile level and the Network/Plant level.

The first issue observed is the absence of any option related to services in the Project Builder user-specific settings. When navigating to Project Builder and accessing the user-specific options, only standard processable objects such as materials and activities are available. No setting allows services or lean services to be defined as processable objects.

The second issue occurs during the creation of a Purchase Requisition from a material component using Lean Service ( materials of type SRV with Product Type Group 2 ). When attempting to generate the PR, the system displays an error message requesting the entry of a “Validity Start Date.” However, this field is not available or visible in the Project Builder interface for either the component or the related network activity, making it impossible for the user to provide the required information.

This behavior is observed exclusively for Lean Services, as non stocked material components do not trigger the same error and function correctly.                  

Could you please advise if there might be something missing in my configuration that is preventing this functionality on my system?

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: PLMSI 3.0 FP04 – What You Need to Know About the Latest Release
entry.content:
Another year has started and we have seen the birth of another feature pack for PLM System Integration for SAP S/4HANA.

Find out what's new and why it matters.

Extended characteristic mapping for Product and Document exchange outbound scenario

Release 3.0 FP04 introduces a more flexible outbound mapping layer for product versions (PV) and document versions (DV).
In addition to class-based characteristic exchange, consultants can now configure:

  • Outbound class assignments, including class numbers and class types, per product version
  • Mappings of class characteristics to target fields in the Domain Model, or to extension fields of the product version
  • Conversion routines, default values, and value mapping tables

This gives solution architects a finer‑grained control mechanism to harmonize master data between SAP and external PLM systems, which proves very useful in multi-system engineering landscapes.

For more information, see Map DM Product Version to SAP Material Master.

Likewise, this feature is available for Document Version Integration. 

For more information, see Map DM Document Version to SAP Document Info Record.

Outbound maintenance requests for classification changes

FP04 adds the ability to trigger outbound maintenance requests for PV as material master, DV as document info record, CO as change request, and CO as engineering change master, based on:

  • Class assignments
  • Characteristic value updates
  • Value changes 

Change Object Integration: In the new view Define Classification Conditions for Outbound in the Customizing for Map DM Change Object to SAP Change Master or Map DM Change Object to SAP Change Record, you can now control which characteristics trigger outbound data transfer to an external PLM system when updated in the SAP system. Specify a Class Number, Class Type, and Characteristic to define outbound-relevant classification conditions.

For more information, see Map DM Change Object to SAP Change Master or Map DM Change Object to SAP Change Record.

The same feature is available for Document Version Integration (see Map DM Document Version to SAP Document Info Record) and for Product Version Integration (see Map DM Product Version to SAP Material Master).

For integration architects, this significantly improves synchronization accuracy and reduces the chances of missed updates for downstream processes.

Product and structure version compatibility support in version-controlled BOMs

 
PLMSI now supports product versioning at both header and item level for version-controlled BOMs.
With the new BOM Header Unlimited Validity checkbox, consultants can enable perpetual header validity (defaulting to Jan 1, 1900), simplifying BOM maintenance and reducing the need for repeated header creation. This is particularly relevant for customers with high-frequency engineering iterations and complex BOM structures.
 

For more information, see Map DM Structure Version to SAP Classic BOM.

 

Transfer of iPPE: PVS from an external PLM system

Feature Pack 04 expands structure integration capabilities by adding support for transferring Integrated Product and Process Engineering (iPPE) – Product Variant Structures (PVS). You can now configure inbound processing for:

  • Access nodes & variants
  • Structure nodes & node relationships
  • Component variants

A new integration pattern (SAP_SVasPPECMPPVS) and several supporting BAdIs provide robust extension points for customizing the mapping logic. 

For more information, see Map DM Structure Version to SAP CMP Product Variant Structure.

What Else is New in PLM system integration for SAP S/4HANA?

The release delivers additional BAdIs across POL, SV, and change handling scenarios.
Notably, you can now leverage the MPO Value Control Structure, which boosts flexibility when working with extension fields and custom mappings. For teams implementing tailored integration logic, this significantly improves maintainability and robustness. For detailed information, see our What's New Viewer. Please make sure to select the latest version 3.0 FP04 to filter for the most recent updates.

Expand Your Range

We are happy and proud that another members of our PLM system integration family has released a new versions of its product:

PLM system integration, add-on for 3DEXPERIENCE has published its new version 3.3. Check out the Release Notes.

Business Content

As for each feature pack, we have updated our comprehensive set of business content (BC) sets to help you configure your system with ease. For details, see Initial Configuration.

For a comprehensive list of our new and enhanced features for release 3.0 FP04, check out our What's New Viewer.

All relevant information on PLM system integration is also available via the links below:

  • SAP Help Portal: PLM system integration for SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP Product Availability Matrix (SAP login required): Matrix for PLM system integration 3.0 for SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP Roadmap Explorer: PLM system integration

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date to our latest enablement assets: linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo

entry.title: Automating Invoice Predictions from SAP Databricks with SAP Datasphere Task Chain
entry.content:
In our previous blog, we demonstrated how to process semi-structured PDF invoices, parse them and store the results in a Delta table before applying machine learning algorithms to predict payment delays. These predictions are hosted in SAP Databricks as part of a customer managed data product, with results written to a target Delta table in Unity Catalog and shared via Delta Sharing with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC).

This is how our customer managed data product holding the first processed 1.000 invoices looks like before any updates: 

The next challenge on our journey was automation. Once new invoices land in the S3 bucket, we need a fully automated process that parses them, updates predictions, and refreshes the Delta share, without any manual intervention. Since SAP consumes the data federatively, business users expect up‑to‑date predictions in near real time.

To meet this requirement, my colleague @Mostafa_Shaarawy1 and Idesigned and implemented an end‑to‑end orchestration flow using SAP Datasphere (DSP) Task Chains and Databricks API to:
  • trigger the Databricks notebook on schedule or on demand,
  • monitor its execution,
  • and ensure updated predictions flow instantly to SAP BDC.

To achieve this, we are going to utilize the run-now and the get run APIs described in Databricks API reference, with authentication handled via a Databricks Service Principle.

The result: a fully automated pipeline that delivers fresh, reliable insights to business users, improving decision‑making, operational efficiency, and payment‑risk visibility.

Business Use Case: Finance Manager’s Perspective

Imagine you’re a finance manager responsible for optimizing cash flow. You rely on accurate predictions of payment delays to decide which customers to follow up with and when. With Datasphere orchestration, the moment new invoices arrive, the system automatically processes them, updates predictions, and makes the latest insights available in SAP BDC, without any manual steps. This means you can act faster, reduce overdue payments, and improve working capital, all while saving time and effort.

We’ll achieve this using Datasphere Task Chains, leveraging their API Task (REST) capability and a Generic HTTP connection to seamlessly integrate with SAP Databricks.

Disclaimer: The scenario we are covering in this blog is technically working fine but is not officially supported in the SAP Databricks documentation.

Solution Overview

The architecture looks like this:

What makes this work?

  • The architecture consists of three components:

    • DSP Task Chains orchestrate Databricks jobs using REST API calls.
    • Databricks Jobs API triggers and monitors the notebook execution.
    • Delta Sharing + Unity Catalog exposes the updated prediction table live to SAP BDC/DSP.

This ensures new data flows from invoice ingestion → prediction → Delta share → SAP consumption end‑to‑end, without manual steps.

Prerequisites

Before building the orchestration, we need to first complete these steps:

  1. Create a Service Principal in SAP Databricks Workspace at the workspace level to generate:

    • Client ID
    • Client Secret
    • OAuth Token Endpoint.

  2. Assign the service principal permissions to run the job and access the target Delta table.

    • This avoids using personal tokens and ensures secure automated API calls.

  3. Prepare the Databricks Job

    • Identify the Job ID of the notebook that:
      • Processes new invoices,
      • Runs ML predictions,
      • Writes results into the target Delta table in Unity Catalog.

  4. Update the Delta Share and the Share CSN document

Step-by-Step Implementation

1) Set-up Databricks and create service Principal

A service principal in Databricks is a dedicated, non-human identity built for automation. It enables secure, API‑only access to Databricks resources for scripts, automated workflows, and CI/CD pipelines, all without depending on personal user credentials.

a- Go to Settings > Ìdentity and Access > Service Principals > Add service principal:

b- Generate a secret for this Service Principal and copy it as well as the Client ID (you'll need them for the definition of the Httpconnection in

SAP Datasphere😞

c- Go to the notebook under Scheduleand create a new one:

d- Switch to the Jobs & Pipelines section and select your scheduled job and copy the job ID (you'll need it in the API Invocationrequest body of the task chain in SAP Datasphere to call the right job): 

2) Set-up your Http Connection in your Datasphere space

  • In your space, navigate to Connections in Datasphere and choose Http as connection type.

  • Enter the connection details.
Host   = <your_tenant_url without 'https://' >.
Port    = 443
Protocol     = HTTPS

Use Cloud Connector
    = False

Authentication Type
     = OAuth2.0

OAuth Grant Type
 = Client Credentials

OAuth Token Endpoint = <Your_token_Url_endpoint>

OAuth Scope = all-apis

OAuth Response Type: Token

OAuth Token Request Content Type: URL Encoded

Client ID: <Client_Id_of_DBX_Service_Principle>

Secret: <Secret_of_DBX_Service_Principle>

  • Save the connection.

3) Create Task Chain with API Task

  • Navigate to Data builder and create a new Task Chain.

  • From the top of the canvas, drag and drop API Task to the editor.

From the properties of the Task Chain, select under Connection the Http connection created in step 2.

In the API Invocation section, choose POST method and the mode to be Asynchronous.

  • The base url will be automatically generated from the connection details
  • In the API Path, write run-now (more information can be found in the Databricks API documentation https://docs.databricks.com/api/workspace/jobs/runnow).
  • The body should be a json object with the job-idof the Databricks job.
  • Choose Get result from HTTP status code and response body and set the path to run_id. This will map the property run_id in the API response to be used for status retrieval to be able to monitor the Databricks job from the task chain.

In the Status API section, we will use the get single run API for status retrieval as documented in Databricks API documentation https://docs.databricks.com/api/workspace/jobs/getrun.

  • Set the API path to runs/get?run_id={id}. Since we have, in the previous step, configured the path to run-id, the API task will dynamically use its value in the query parameter during run time.

  • In the response section choose Get result from HTTP status code and response body to determine whether the databricks job was successful or not, based on the result from the response body.
  • As success indicator use: status.termination_details.code equals to SUCCESS
  • As error indicator use: status.stateequals to TERMINATED

 

4) Run the API task to trigger the Databricks job

Now the API task is ready to be triggered. Once triggered the Invocation API will be called with the job-id in the request body and the status API will be called periodically to evaluate the completion of the Databricks job.

For testing purpose, you can conveniently just run the API task without having to run the task chain. Click on Test Run in the API task to run it.

You can now click on the Data Integration Monitor icon to see the run details.

When you switch to the monitoring, you can then see the run details of the job:

This is also reflected in SAP Databricks:

Once the job has completed successfully, you can see the 5 invoices newly added to the S3 bucket now updated in the custom managed data product shared from SAP Databricks to SAP Business Data Cloud:

Key Benefits for Business Users

  • Real-time insights: Data is refreshed immediately as new invoices are processed.
  • Zero manual effort: A fully automated end‑to‑end pipeline.
  • Improved cash flow management: Faster decisions supported by fresh insights.
  • Operational efficiency: No delays between data processing and business consumption.
  • Federated access: Data is consumed directly in SAP BDC—no physical copies or complex integrations required.

entry.title: Re: FAGL_FCV-Posting period 002 2026 is not open
entry.content: Hi, Please run transaction OB52 and check: Which authorization group is open for period 002/2026. Whether company code 1600 is included in that group. Whether the ledger group for the non-leading ledger is explicitly opened for 1600. If you confirm that OB52 is open for 1600 but the error persists, then the issue is likely with valuation area assignment or fiscal year variant mismatch.

entry.title: Re: Working with SAP SDK for Android and older Android devices with DigiCert G5 Certificate
entry.content:
We were already aware of potential issues due to the missing G5 certificate, as we recently received reports from a few users with older Android versions who were no longer able to access the SAP Build Work Zone. In these cases, access is provided directly through a web browser. We were able to solve the problem here by having users install the new DigiCert G5 certificate locally on their devices.

After consulting with our Customer Success Manager, SAP also considers this to be a correct solution for cases where the certificate cannot be stored directly in the app itself, as described in this post. Maybe this information will help someone else.

entry.title: Value differences are posted solely to stock account in multiple line invoices
entry.content:
Hi,

in sap, for invoice with multiple line items, line by line quantity is checked against the stock in the time of posting.

This leads to that if there are differences they are posted to stock account instead of the line quantities being accumulated and then posted proportionally to stock account and expense.

How did you tackle this issue in your company?

Thanks,

Golan

entry.title: Re: Italian special depreciation keys for ledger 2L
entry.content:
Hi,

  1. Parallel valuation done by ledgers assumes differences in valuation. Any attempt to synchronize multiple GAAP cross ledgers, e.g. by using the same depreciation keys, does not make any sense in general. 
  2. Especially the depreciation keys in Italy are "special" and typically not applicable in IFRS.. 
  3. Entity "Chart of Depreciation" does not play any vital role in Intelligent Asset Accounting anymore and it will be completely removed in the future.
  4. You can create custom depreciation keys also for IFRS via the process of pre-approved Expert Configuration process via template DPK.

Best regards,

   Mick

entry.title: SAP Joule for Consultants (J4C): A Strategic Overview
entry.content:
SAP Joule for Consultants (J4C): A Strategic Overview

SAP Joule represents a significant advancement in SAP’s generative AI strategy, offering a suite of role-based variants including those for end users, developers, and specific line-of-business processes. Among these, Joule for Consultants (J4C) is uniquely engineered for SAP delivery organizations and system integrators. Operating as a standalone capability within SAP Business AI and powered by SAP BTP, J4C is designed to address the historical fragmentation of SAP knowledge by providing a centralized, conversational interface for expert-curated content.

Positioning of Joule for Consultants within the SAP Business AI ecosystem.

Figure 1: Positioning of SAP Joule for Consultants

Core Differentiators and Technical Grounding

Unlike generic AI tools, J4C is trained and grounded in SAP’s authoritative, non-public knowledge assets. This ensures that recommendations remain aligned with "clean core" principles and current product roadmaps.

  • Exclusive Knowledge Base: J4C utilizes terabytes of gated content, including SAP Notes, Knowledge Base Articles (KBAs), and SAP Activate implementation guides.
  • Methodological Context: The system natively understands consulting terminology, such as SAP Activate phases and Fit-to-Standard constructs.
  • Code Intelligence: It leverages a fine-tuned ABAP model trained on millions of lines of code to explain legacy logic and identify dependencies during system conversions.

The J4C reasoning layer integrates diverse SAP data sources to support consultant prompts.

Figure 2: J4C knowledge flow

Strategic Benefits and Implementation Considerations:

Integrating J4C into delivery methodologies offers several measurable advantages, though it requires careful organizational planning.

Key Benefits:

  • Accelerated Delivery: Reduces the time required to locate authoritative answers during discovery and design.
  • Risk Mitigation: Grounding responses in SAP best practices minimizes the likelihood of design deviations and subsequent rework.
  • Scalability: Acts as a continuous enablement layer, allowing junior consultants to access senior-level insights without constant escalation.
  • Standardization: Ensures a consistent interpretation of SAP guidance across global engagements.

Critical Considerations:

  • Commercial Readiness: Deployment requires specific SAP AI Unit entitlements and technical BTP configuration.
  • Accountability: J4C serves as a guide; final design decisions and project governance remain the responsibility of the consultant.
  • Dynamic Coverage: While extensive, the depth of information may vary by specific solution area and release version.

SAP J4C in action:

  1. Log on to SAP J4C as shown:

Figure 3: Login

  1. Ask relevant query:

Figure 4: Query

  1. The answer as shown:

Figure 4: Response

  1. Grounded in SAP Data:

Figure 5: Guardrails and Grounding

Future Evolution

The roadmap for J4C focuses on transitioning from a conversational assistant to an agentic consulting copilot. This evolution includes automated guidance for SAP Activate deliverables and proactive design validation. 

In summary, J4C is an essential tool for the modern SAP consultant, providing a structural advantage in speed, quality, and consistency for complex enterprise transformations.

entry.title: Triggers no SAP B1
entry.content:
Bom dia, pessoal, tudo bem?

Possuo uma arquitetura de ingestão de dados em tempo quase real do SAP Business One (rodando em SAP HANA) para um Data Mesh no Google BigQuery, utilizando Apache Kafka como camada de streaming.

Uso como critério, os campos de UpdateDate = CURRENTDATE.

Porém, quando falamos na JDT1 (faço uma query linkando OJDT e JDT1 para pegar o UpdateDate) , temos rotinas internas que atualizam campos como BalDueDeb e BalDueCred, ou seja, essas alterações via rotinas SAP não me geram uma alteração no UpdateDate, e por consequência meu BigQuery fica com dados desatualizados/incorretos para aquele TransID.

Gostaria de saber se criar uma trigger para pegar todos meus Updates realizados na JDT1 e enviar para uma tabela de auditoria:

  • Seria aceito pelo SAP? O que o SAP diz a respeito de criação de triggers? 
  • Eu poderia ter problemas futuros?
  • Eu geraria muito impacto em meu banco de dados? Em performance no sistema, consumo de recursos.. (a trigger seria executada a cada update e/ou insert)
  • Quais outras saídas posso ter? 
  • Se eu criar uma view no meu Hana para "simular" essas alterações de BalDueDeb e BalDueCred através das baixas realizadas, resolveria meu problema?

Atenciosamente,

entry.title: Re: Creating a Single Warehouse Order for Multiple Custom...
entry.content:
1. You can trigger WT manually  for the 10 Delvieries together or setup the PPF actions (you can use std SAP  0DWT condition type ...with access seq 0D01 and maintain condition records using  t-code /scwm/dlvppfc

2. For WOCR setup, for config of  " Define Limit Values"   Create a limit profile where the "Max. Number of Items" is set to 10 or higher and assign to your custom WOCR. 

3. YOu have two ways to determine this WOCR. First,  by Defining  Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas, assign all the Activity areas where these 10 Deliveries will be picked from, to the same Activity and WOCR . So, if you have 5 AA's in total. you need to make 5 entries in this config. 

Alternatively, you can assign WOCR to the main WPT you are using for the outbound process.

entry.title: Why SAP Processes in Brazil Are So Complex — and How S/4HANA Localization Solves It
entry.content:
Running business operations in Brazil requires navigating one of the most complex tax and regulatory landscapes in the world. For companies implementing or operating SAP S/4HANA, understanding why the process is so complicated—and how SAP’s Brazil Localization supports compliance—is essential for success.

This blog explores:

  • Why Brazil’s tax and legal environment is uniquely challenging
  • How SAP S/4HANA handles Brazilian tax processes
  • Key configuration steps and components
  • The tools SAP provides for Brazil Localization
  • How these capabilities help companies stay compliant while reducing effort and risk

1. Why Is the SAP Process So Complicated in Brazil?

Brazil has an extremely complex tax system with multiple layers of taxation, each defined by different government bodies (federal, state, and municipal). Unlike many countries where taxes are more standardized and centralized, Brazil’s fiscal environment is characterized by:

● Multiple Tax Types

  • ICMS – State VAT-like tax
  • IPI – Federal excise tax
  • PIS/COFINS – Federal social contributions
  • ISS – Municipal services tax
  • II – Import tax

Each tax has its own rules, rates, credits, exemptions, and cascading calculations.

● Significant Variations by State and Municipality

Brazil has 27 states and 5,500+ cities, each potentially defining:

  • Different tax rates
  • Different fiscal codes
  • Different calculation methods

This results in thousands of possible tax scenarios.

● Mandatory Electronic Documents

Brazil mandates real‑time communication with government systems:

  • NF-e – Electronic invoice for goods
  • NFS-e – Electronic service invoice
  • CT-e – Transport documents
  • MDFe – Manifest document
  • Reinf / eSocial – Tax and labor reporting

SAP must integrate with all these services using specified schemas, requirements, and government-approved formats.

● Constant Regulatory Changes

Brazil updates tax rules frequently. Companies need systems that can adapt quickly.

This complexity makes Brazil one of the most difficult countries for ERP compliance—and explains why SAP requires specialized localization components.

2. How Brazil Tax Rules Work Inside SAP S/4HANA

SAP does not use the “standard tax procedure” for Brazil. Instead, it uses Brazil-specific tax engines and frameworks that reflect local legal requirements.

Here’s how it works:

a. Tax Calculation via Tax Service / TAXBRA

Brazil uses the TAXBRA tax engine (or Advanced Tax features in S/4HANA) to automatically determine:

  • Tax type
  • Tax rate
  • Base amount
  • Tax calculation formula
  • CST codes / CFOP codes
  • Credits and debits (PIS, COFINS, ICMS)

It evaluates all components based on materials, customers, operations, and legal scenario.

b. CFOP Determination (Fiscal Operation Codes)

CFOP defines the purpose of each movement (sale, return, transfer, import, etc.).
SAP automatically assigns CFOP based on:

  • Movement type
  • Document type
  • Partner type
  • Location of origin/destination

c. NFe & NFSe Processing

Brazil’s electronic invoices must be validated by the government before goods can move.

SAP supports:

  • XML generation
  • Digital signing
  • Transmission to SEFAZ (state government)
  • Error handling and reprocessing
  • Contingency modes
  • DANFE printing

This integrates into S/4HANA via the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) solution.

d. Nota Fiscal (Legal Document)

In Brazil, every business transaction must generate a Nota Fiscal.
SAP uses:

  • J1B1N / S/4HANA equivalents for document creation
  • NF-e XML generation
  • Legal numbering
  • Tax books integration

e. SPED Reports

Brazil requires SPED fiscal reporting:

  • SPED Fiscal (ICMS/IPI)
  • SPED Contribuições (PIS/COFINS)
  • ECD / ECF

SAP generates the required files using Brazil Localization extensions.

3. Configuring Brazil Localization in SAP S/4HANA

Key configuration areas include:

1. Activate Brazil Localization

Enable localizations through Country-Specific Functions for Brazil.

2. Define Tax Types, Rates, and Formulas

Configure:

  • ICMS, IPI, PIS, COFINS
  • Tax tables
  • CST codes
  • CFOP
  • Base type formulas

Tax code logic usually involves condition-based tax determination.

3. Configure Nota Fiscal

Set up:

  • Document types
  • Number ranges
  • Partner functions
  • Freight, insurance, and other NF components

4. Activate DRC for Brazil

Configure SAP DRC (formerly GRC NFe) for:

  • NF-e
  • CT-e
  • MDF-e
  • Reinf
  • eSocial

This requires:

  • Certificates
  • XML schemas
  • Integration with SEFAZ and Receita Federal

5. Set Up SPED Reporting

Define:

  • Blocks
  • Tax books
  • Mapping of fields
  • Layout generation

6. Integration with Logistics & Finance

Brazil localization impacts:

  • SD (Sales)
  • MM (Purchasing)
  • FI (Tax accounting)
  • LE (Delivery / Transportation)

All processes must be aligned.

4. Tools SAP Offers to Support Brazil Localization

SAP provides specialized tools to simplify compliance:

1. SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC / eDocument Framework)

Supports electronic document creation and communication:

  • NF-e
  • NFS-e
  • CT-e
  • MDF-e
  • Reinf
  • eSocial

SAP DRC ensures real‑time compliance and automates all government communication.

2. SAP Tax Service for Brazil

Cloud-based service that centralizes:

  • Tax rules
  • Rates
  • Formulas
  • CFOP/CST determination

Reduces manual maintenance and ensures consistent tax calculation.

3. SAP Localization Hub

Central platform for country-specific compliance resources:

  • Tax updates
  • Legal changes
  • Templates

4. SAP SPED Generator

Automates SPED Fiscal & SPED Contribution files.

5. SAP Notes & Localization Updates

Brazil requires continuous updates, and SAP delivers them through:

  • SAP Notes
  • Support Packages
  • Legal change announcements

5. Conclusion: Brazil Localization in SAP Is Complex, but Manageable With the Right Tools

Brazil’s tax system is one of the most challenging in the world—and SAP must handle thousands of tax scenarios, frequent legal changes, and real‑time government integration.

The good news: SAP S/4HANA offers a comprehensive localization framework with automated tax engines, NF-e integration, SPED reports, and continuous updates to keep companies compliant.

When configured correctly, these tools reduce manual effort, avoid tax penalties, and streamline operations across logistics, finance, and compliance.

entry.title: Train-the-Trainers Hands-on Workshop: SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP)
entry.content:
Train-the-Trainers IBP

Join the exclusive hands-on workshop on SAP IBP for university professors with full membership in the SAP University Alliances program, taking place in March 2026.

 

This exclusive training introduces SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP), the successor solution to SAP SCM. SAP IBP is a strategic business planning platform designed to integrate and optimize supply chain processes, enabling organizations to respond agilely to market dynamics and customer demands. The sessions will be led by Marcela Revilla, Senior SAP Consultant Instructor, a highly experienced professional with multiple SAP certifications across various applications.

 

Participate in our webinar designed for faculty members from universities with full membership in the SAP University Alliances program, delivered by experienced instructors, and discover how SAP IBP can strengthen the teaching of planning and supply chain management. During the training, the current state of the solution, its key functionalities, and its practical application in an academic environment will be presented.

 

Throughout the event, participants will learn about:

  • Introduction to SAP IBP: Overview of the solution, architecture, and key application areas.

  • Evaluation Phase Results: Analysis of the preparation and adoption process of SAP IBP.

  • Pilot Environment and Pilot Project: Practical demonstration of SAP IBP in real teaching scenarios.

 

Date: March 23 and 24
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 1:00 p.m. (GMT-6)

 

Participation is free of charge and exclusively open to faculty members from educational institutions with full membership in the SAP University Alliances program, with registration using a valid academic email address. The training will be held online, subject to a minimum number of participants, and is sponsored by SAP University Alliances.

 

Register now!

 

Don’t miss this unique opportunity to take your teaching methods to the next level. We look forward to your participation!

Current state:

  • We’re doing mass imports into Employee Central (EC) with acquired team member data.
  • Onboarding paperwork is still paper-based, which is creating a significant burden for our business partners.

Goal:
Leverage technology so that onboarding paperwork is triggered automatically (instead of relying on manual/paper processes).

Question:
Is there a way to mass import acquired team members directly into Onboarding 2.0 (or bulk-trigger the onboarding workflow/documents) after they’re loaded into EC?

I’ve found the “Add New Hire to Onboarding” option for acquired team members, but it appears to be one person at a time, which won’t scale for acquisitions.

If anyone has guidance, documentation, or a recommended approach (e.g., an integration, template/import method, or best practice), I’d really appreciate it. Thank you!

entry.title: The Top 5 Things You Always Wanted to Know About Situation Handling
entry.content:
Situation Handling let’s you alert appropriate users about critical business issues requiring their attention. If you're curious about how it works or need more details, this blog post is for you. Additionally, we've enhanced our documentation - be sure to take a look here: Documentation.

1)    How does it really work?

Situation Handling detects business issues in your systems. The detection works for issues with either business objects or error and warning messages. In the following, these will simply be referred to as “objects”. An issue is typically defined by specific conditions or events happening that are assigned to the objects.  – and then, voilà, either a routine daily batch run or business event monitoring identifies the objects with issues.* It can be, for example, an expiring sales contract, an overbooked flight, and many more. This leads to the next question:

2)    How do I specify the business issues to be addressed by Situation Handling? How is the setup done?

Let’s understand this first: Situation Handling can detect issues from only one object or between two associated objects. If only one object is involved, that’s quite easy – the object triggering an issue, with certain conditions or events, is the very same object that helps to solve it by changing itself in a specific app. One example would be a sales quotation that has expired and needs to be checked for purchase details in order to get back to the customers.

How two interfering objects work: One is usually triggering the situation. For example, it can be triggered because it’s newly created, extends a certain value, or expires. The other object is the one that needs to be changed because it’s the one affected. This is also called the anchor object, as it “anchors” the situation. For example, a change in the processor may cause a purchasing document item to require a review. Alternatively, missing supplier confirmations make operational purchasers check the schedule lines of purchase order items again.

For the Situation Handling engine to detect situations, it needs to know what to look for. The objects with their conditions, events, and possible mappings between them need to be defined. Your business area may have very specific business issues – we got you covered with a wide range of ready-to-use situation templates. They are called “templates” because elements (like conditions) are predefined, while others (like condition filters or your specific situation texts) can be adapted by you. Just copy a template into your personal situation type and enable it.

I can already anticipate your next question: What if my case is not on the list? No problem at all – with our new framework, you can define all parts of Situation Handling yourself. It’s called “extended framework” because of its extended functionalities.

3)    And how do I alert someone? This seems complicated.

I’m not gonna lie, it seems complicated. It’s seems complicated at first, because we’ve provided all the different ways to alert someone that we (and you!) could possibly think of. First, your company’s organizational structure – a lot of customers want to alert specific team members based on their job functions. But we also offer the possibility to alert people depending on the data a situation provides – for example, the creator of a sales order, its processor, or other field values. For more information, check out the documentation.

4)    Let me try it 🤩

Now you must be wondering: Can I check this out? Sure – all SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customers have Situation Handling included. Just assign the “Business Process Specialist” role to your user and open the “Manage Situation Types” app. Our situation template “Sales Order Is Close to Expiry” is auto-activated, which means you can immediately check it out under “Situation Types”.

If you want to try setting up something very simple, we recommend following the test script from SAP Best Practices of our Situation Handling scope item (31N)  under “Accelerators”. If you’re more interested in our extended framework, we’ve even built three demo situation templates and our own demo app for creating dummy data. Check it out in this blog post.

5)    Uff… I have issues (and I don’t mean my business issues 😫). I did all the setup, and Situation Handling doesn’t seem to work. No one gets alerted.

No worries at all. There’s already a very well-written blog post that provides a lot of guidance.

6)    What next?

Interested? Great. We have a lot more to offer.

  • Let’s get started 😊 Check out all situation templates to see if any fit your business issues. For the setup, check out Getting Started.
  • Already a pro? Check out how you can improve your Situation Handling by monitoring it and changing certain parameters in this blog post.
  • Interested in AI? Stay tuned 😎
  • Need more help with Responsibility Management and alerting relevant users? There’s also a blog post series available.
  • Start creating your custom use cases (if you haven’t done so already): Blog post

Last but not least: I’m one of your technical writers for Situation Handling. Not only do I provide you with six steps when you only expected five, I’m also always happy to help 🫡This is our documentation (quick link: http://help.sap.com/s4_ce_situationhandling for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and http://help.sap.com/s4_op_situationhandling for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition/SAP S/4HANA) and we love to hear your feedback.

Don’t forget to comment on anything you didn’t get, missed, would have loved to explore further – I promise, we will react! And of course, comment on all the things you liked – then we know what to keep. Thank you 🫶

More questions? Comment on this blog post, and we will try to answer them 😊

@Christiane_Poreski 
@angelika_salmen 
@sebastian_doeweling 
@axel_spriestersbach 
@Dr_Joerg_Rett 
@Vidya_Shetty1 
@NisargaPuttegowda 

_____

* For sure, monitoring business events is way more elegant than a batch run, as it catches issues right away (with some delay due to system load though, but still fairly recent). But obviously, not all conditions can be detected through events, so a once-a-day batch run is performed on the objects to identify those with issues. A batch run also has its advantages, for example, it sends controlled information once a day with no spam by frequent events.

entry.title: Why are Business Partner roles split into Procurement vs Finance (Supplier/Customer)
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I have a question about Business Partner master data in SAP (BP approach). When creating a Supplier or Customer, I see that the roles are split into separate areas, for example:

  • Supplier – Procurement / Purchasing (FLCU01) ,(FLVN01)

  • Supplier – Finance / Financial Accounting (FLVN00), (FLCU00),
    (and similarly for Customer)

From what I observed:

  • If I assign the Procurement role, the system expects/needs the Finance role as well (which makes sense, because purchasing usually ends with invoicing and payment).

  • But I can assign the Finance role without assigning the Procurement role.

My questions:

  1. What is the business logic / real-life use case for having a Supplier/Customer with Finance-only role (no Procurement/Sales role)? In which scenarios would a company want this?

  2. Why did SAP design it as two separate roles instead of having one combined role that includes both procurement + finance, with optional sections/fields?

entry.title: Re: QA32 : Material return after invoice reception
entry.content:
In ECC6, the error you’re seeing (M7 021 – Deficit of PU IR quantity) is standard system behavior: once the vendor invoice has been posted, the system prevents you from returning the goods via QA32/MIGO with a “return to vendor” posting, because the invoice quantity is already matched against the GR.

Why it happens

You received the material into quality inspection stock. The vendor invoice was posted before the quality decision. When you try to post a usage decision in QA32 with “Return to Vendor,” the system checks the GR/IR balance. Since the invoice is already posted, the system sees a deficit and raises M7 021.

Standard SAP Options

Reverse the invoice first
Cancel the vendor invoice (MR8M) so that the GR/IR balance is restored. Then process QA32 with “Return to Vendor.” After the return, re‑post the invoice if needed.

Post return delivery with reference to PO
Use MIGO with movement type 122 (Return to Vendor) against the original PO. This is the standard way to return goods after invoice receipt. The system will create a credit memo request to clear the vendor invoice. If invoice cannot be reversed (period closed)

Post a manual adjustment:
Use a return delivery (122) in the current open period. Finance posts a manual credit/debit note to adjust vendor liability. This ensures stock and FI are aligned, even though the invoice was already booked.

Suggested Action

  1. Check with Finance whether the invoice can be reversed (MR8M). If yes, that’s the cleanest solution.
  2. If reversal is not possible (period closed), use MIGO 122 return delivery and process a vendor credit memo manually.

entry.title: Re: Why are Business Partner roles split into Procurement...
entry.content:
Why SAP split Supplier/Customer into Procurement vs. Finance roles is because

Procurement/Sales roles (FLVN01, FLCU01)
These are operational roles. They hold data needed for day‑to‑day transactions like purchase orders, RFQs, or sales orders (e.g., purchasing organization, order currency, incoterms).

Finance roles (FLVN00, FLCU00)
These are accounting roles. They hold data needed for invoice posting, payments, dunning, reconciliation (e.g., reconciliation account, payment terms, tolerance groups).

SAP deliberately separated them because not every business partner needs both sets of data. This modular design allows flexibility and avoids cluttering the master record with irrelevant fields.

Real‑life use cases for Finance‑only roles

One‑time vendors/customers for manual postings
A company may need to record a payment, refund, or adjustment in FI without ever issuing a PO or sales order.

Employees as vendors/customers
For travel reimbursements or payroll advances, employees are created as vendors in FI only. No procurement data is needed.

Tax authorities or government agencies
You may need to post payments (taxes, fees) or receive refunds, but you’ll never issue a PO or sales order.

Intercompany clearing accounts
Some entities are set up as customers/vendors purely for FI postings to handle intercompany balances, not for operational procurement/sales.

Why not one combined role?

Flexibility: Keeps BP lean. You only assign what’s needed.

Governance: Different teams own different data. Procurement teams maintain purchasing data; Finance teams maintain accounting data. Splitting roles enforces clear responsibility.

Compliance: Some partners must exist only in FI (e.g., tax offices). If SAP forced procurement fields, you’d have redundant or meaningless data.

Extensibility: Modular roles make it easier to add new partner functions in future without redesigning the whole BP model.

entry.title: A SAP BTP Kyma Encryption/Decryption Microservice for ALL Contexts (e.g. SAP Datasphere/BDC/ABAP)
entry.content:
G’day!

Warning: This is only for readers who are really interested in this subject 😍 The article is quite comprehensive and has got some cross-article links inside, that also need to be considered. Therefore: A lot of reading and a lot of hands-on + thinking if you want to rebuild the thing.​

Below is the structure of this article:

  • 1 Status Quo on En-/Decryption in BTP
  • 2 Motivation
  • 3 Fundamentals 
  • 4 Architecture​
  • 5 HDLFS Configuration
  • 6 Insights into Python
  • 7 Containerization & Deployment in Kyma
  • 8 Achievements
  • 9 More Scope
  • 10 References

Let's begin:

1 Status Quo on En-/Decryption in BTP

When it comes to The following outlines the situation, platform and application-wise:

  • Individual applications (e.g. SAP Successfactors) got proprietary encryption/decryption mechanisms available. They are typically not reusable.
  • SAP Integration Suite has got PGP En-/Decryptor nodes available. However, it is not advisable to run large volume scenarios using this approach. It has been built for data sizes on the levels represented within message handling scenarios (I‘m not an expert therein, but I guess thats typically not in the area of GBs).
  • A policy/rule for data to remain encrypted until its landing into BTP: You face challenges to securely onboard large volume files containing sensitive data into BTP, because there is no out-of-the-box solution to decrypt them at rest once available in BTP (and vice versa for an outbound flow).

The Challenge

2 Motivation

​Due to the above limitations, we were eager to find an appropriate solution approach that first of all satifies our specific scenario, but can also be augmented to other contexts. This is why the following motivation arose:

  • Especially En-/Decryption for larger files on BTP side seems to be a missing piece in the puzzle.
  • Our ambition is to encapsulate such kind of mechanism centrally as a reusable service. Multiple kinds of contexts can be served, exposing this service: Applications, data flows & transformations etc. – in our context the consumer will be SAP Datasphere.
  • There is another consequence of finally being able to load decrypted large data sets into BTP. To be more precise: To onboard this data into SAP Datasphere to further process and transform, the fact that Base64 encoded data must be handled in our context too, why not also expose or incorporate columnar Base64 En-/Decoding into the microservice?
  • The intended solution is custom-built and must be managed individually. However, the baseline that will be set can be reused and adapted to various customer needs and application contexts.

3 Fundamentals 

​The following represent some fundamentals and success criteria we‘ve established for our solution:

  • Use HANA Data Lake Files (HDLFS) as a file store for inbound/outbound persistency
  • Implement the service as containerized Python code logic
  • Service capabilities must be controllable and schedulable within Kyma
  • Service can handle PGP encrypted files, but can also decrypt for outbound delivery
  • Service can decode Base64 columns for inbound source files
  • Service manages and cleans up directories and file in-/output on HDLFS
  • Service can handle files with sizes in GB range
  • Deploy service into a Kyma Cluster (Cloud Foundry? -> no, Memory/Sizing limitations)
  • Security: Facilitate simple and secure handling of all required secrets and certificates
  • Service not exposed via HTTP endpoints (this is kind of a contradiction to a true microservice, however this requirement applies in our context. Under "More Scope" you will find guidelines/directions how to enable HTTP based microservices)

4 Architecture​

The following architectural components represent what we‘ve picked out for our solution:

  • Usage of HANA Data Lake Files (HDLFS) as a file store for inbound/outbound persistency
  • Use BTP Kyma for service runtime (Kyma deployed in the 2nd smallest T-Shirt size)
  • SAP Datasphere represents the persistency layer (DSP as a consumer is exchangeable but in our case relevant for the business scenario)
  • The other more general blue box "other BTP Solutions" indicates, that the service implementation on Kyma isn't necessary limited to SAP Datasphere only, but should be rather considered as something agnostic. This of course heavily depends how it is built and finally implemented.

BTP Solution Architecture Kyma Microservice

5 HDLFS Configuration

I strongly recommend to read the tutorial by Jason Hinsperger to familiarize yourself with the HDLFS REST API dependencies and the essential steps to spin up the instance. You must follow all the steps described therein.

Its important to note that the generated client key and client certificate is what you are about to use from python level. Moreover you should take note of the REST API endpoint. This is the endpoint you run all your requests against (you find it in HANA Cloud Central -> Instances -> HDLFS Instance -> "Files REST API Endpoint".).

To familiarize yourself in general with the HDLFS REST API documentation, you can use this link.

HDLFS REST API Endpoint

One essential detail I don't want to hide, is the IP whitelisting of the Kyma environment. In order to do that, navigate to the start page of your cluster dashboard. There is a section called "Cluster Overview --> Metadata". Copy the (typically three) IP listed there: "NAT Gateway IP Addresses".

Go to your HANA Cloud Central and navigate to your HDLFS instance. Click "Manage Configuration" and go to the section "Connections": If you have enabled the setting to only allow specific IP addresses, likewise maintain the ones of the Kyma Cluster that you previously copied:

HDLFS Configuration Connections

6 Insights into Python

The python coding part is split into several modules, which are all described in a detailed way further below:

  1. Inbound processing
  2. Outbound processing
  3. Common functions
  4. Crypto function for encryption/decryption

Python Modules Sketch

Inbound Module (1)

The inbound part scans a given datalake path for .gpg files, then decrypts each file locally in parallel using a configurable worker count. After decryption, it samples the CSV (semicolon-delimited), detects which columns contain base64-encoded data, and runs a transformation step to produce a cleaned output file. The transformed result is uploaded to a decrypted output path, and the original encrypted input is moved to an archive folder. It logs success or errors with timing details on file level and exits with a failure code if any file fails.

This code snippet describes the parallel processing of files:

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=MAX_PARALLEL_JOBS) as exe:
        futs = {exe.submit(processFile, f): f for f in files}
        for fut in as_completed(futs):
            f = futs[fut]
            try:
                res = fut.result()
                ok += 1
                print(f"[OK] {res['input']} -> {res['output']} sec={res['sec']:.2f} base64_cols={res['base64_cols']}")
            except Exception as ex:
                err.append((f, str(ex)))
                print(f"[ERR] {f}: {ex}")

Sampel code of processing an individual file:

def processFile(file_name: str):
    t0 = timer()
    out_name = normalize_output_name(file_name)

    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
        enc_path = os.path.join(td, file_name)
        dec_path = os.path.join(td, out_name + ".decrypted.csv")
        out_path = os.path.join(td, out_name)

        download_to_file(f"{PATH_IN_DATALAKE}/{file_name}", enc_path)
        decrypt_symmetric(enc_path, dec_path)

        header, sample = sample_csv(dec_path, delimiter=";")
        base64_cols = detect_base64_columns(out_name, header, sample)

        transform_csv(dec_path, out_path, base64_cols, delimiter=";")

        mkdirs(PATH_OUT_DATALAKE)
        upload_file_chunked(f"{PATH_OUT_DATALAKE}/{out_name}", out_path)

    mkdirs(PATH_ARCHIVE)
    rename(f"{PATH_IN_DATALAKE}/{file_name}", f"{PATH_ARCHIVE}/{file_name}")

    return {"input": file_name, "output": out_name, "base64_cols": base64_cols, "sec": timer() - t0}

Outbound module (2)

It scans the HDLFS outbound folder for .csv files and subdirectories containing Parquet parts, then processes them in parallel using a configurable worker count. CSV files are downloaded locally, then encrypted and uploaded back again to an encrypted outbound path. The originals are moved to an archive folder. Parquet file based directories on HDLFS (when enabled) are downloaded, consolidated into a single CSV using PyArrow, encrypted and uploaded similarly. As a last step, they are marked with an _SUCCESS file to prevent reprocessing and moved to the archive. 

Crypto Module (3)

It provides helper functions for symmetric file encryption and decryption using the gpg command-line tool with an AES-256 cipher. It requires a passphrase supplied via the PASSPHRASE environment variable (from a Kyma Secret). Both encrypt_symmetric and decrypt_symmetric execute GPG in batch mode through subprocess, capturing stdout/stderr and validating the return code.

The decrypt_symmetric function is implemented as follows:

 

def decrypt_symmetric(enc_path: str, dec_path: str):
    _require_passphrase()
    cmd = [
        "gpg", "--batch", "--yes",
        "--pinentry-mode", "loopback",
        "--passphrase", PASSPHRASE,
        "--output", dec_path,
        "--decrypt", enc_path
    ]
    r = subprocess.run(cmd, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE)
    if r.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(f"GPG decrypt failed: {r.stderr.decode('utf-8', 'replace')}")

Common Functions Module (4)

All major control variables are built using environment variables (defined as secrets in Kyma) for endpoint/container, TLS certificates, verification behavior, timeouts, retries, and chunk size.

In one function it builds a reusable SSL context. API requests are wrapped with exponential-backoff retries for transient HTTP statuses (e.g., 429/5xx) and network errors. It provides HDLFS related helpers to list files/directories, check existence, create directories, rename paths, download remote content to disk in chunks, and upload files.

The below part builds the SSL context to connect to HDLFS via its REST API:

def buildSSLContext() -> ssl.SSLContext:
    ctx = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT)
    if os.path.exists(CRT_PATH) and os.path.exists(KEY_PATH):
        ctx.load_cert_chain(certfile=CRT_PATH, keyfile=KEY_PATH)

    if TLS_VERIFY:
        if CA_CERT_PATH:
            ctx.load_verify_locations(CA_CERT_PATH)
        else:
            ctx.load_default_certs()
        ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED
        ctx.check_hostname = True
    else:
        ctx.check_hostname = False
        ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE

    return ctx

This part establishes a connection to the HDLFS REST API endpoint as defined in the environment variable FILES_REST_API:

def _conn():
    if not FILES_REST_API:
        raise RuntimeError("FILES_REST_API is empty (set env FILES_REST_API).")
    return http.client.HTTPSConnection(FILES_REST_API, timeout=HTTP_TIMEOUT, context=_SSL_CTX)

The below function represents the upload (HDLFS REST API PUT) of a file in streaming mode to an HDLFS directory:

def _put_to_location(location: str):
        url = urlparse(location)
        if u.scheme and u.netloc:
            host = url.netloc
            path = (url.path or "/") + (("?" + url.query) if url.query else "")
            conn = http.client.HTTPSConnection(host, timeout=HTTP_TIMEOUT, context=_SSL_CTX)
        else:
            path = location
            conn = _conn()

        try:
            conn.putrequest("PUT", path)
            conn.putheader("x-sap-filecontainer", CONTAINER)
            conn.putheader("Content-Type", "application/octet-stream")
            conn.putheader("Content-Length", str(size))
            conn.putheader("Connection", "close")
            conn.endheaders()

            with open(file_path, "rb") as f:
                while True:
                    chunk = f.read(CHUNK_SIZE)
                    if not chunk:
                        break
                    conn.send(chunk)

            r = conn.getresponse()
            data = r.read()
            status = r.status
            r.close()

            if status in _RETRYABLE_STATUSES:
                raise RetryableHttpError(f"PUT retryable: {status} {data[:200]!r}")
            if status not in (200, 201):
                raise RuntimeError(f"PUT failed {remote_path_no_leading_slash}: {status} {data[:200]!r}")
        finally:
            conn.close()

7 Containerization & Deployment in Kyma

​I won't describe in detail, how the creation of a docker container is made step by step. I just provide some essentials + hints to guarantee that you succeed and don't stumble upon the same issues I had faced. The following prerequisites must be met in order to create a runnable docker container based on your Dockerfile + python code:

  • ​Local installation of docker (Docker Desktop)
  • Docker registry (e.g. GitHub Docker Registry) – this is from where the Pod pulls the docker image
  • kubectl cli
  • IDE such as Visual Studio code

​For an overall procedure, including the part in Kyma, you can refer to this blog entry on SCN. I strongly recommend that you try to rebuild the example the author walks through first, BEFORE you tackle your actual project.

Recommendations out of my personal learnings:

  • Try to not create any Kyma artifacts via the UI, but rather define those within descriptor yaml files of the corresponding kinds (e.g. kind: Deployment or Service or APIRule).
  • ​!!! A minor but very important thing to consider when running the docker build command, run it as follows:
docker build . --tag ghcr.io/<github_user>/<git_repo_name>:latest --platform linux/amd64
  • The deployment.yaml in the blog specified above is based on three Kyma/K8S artifacts that are created: Deployment, Service + APIRule. Which kind of artifacts you actually require strongly depends on your python implementation and how your service/logic runs. In the event of a purely job schedule based execution of your processing logic, you don't need a service or APIRule artifact, of course. Instead, you'd only need a deployment of kind: CronJob. This will spin up all dependent artifacts implicitely. In a CronJob based deployment you will have to specify the following details in the yaml file: scheduling details (time, frequency, time zone), required volumes, container spec including secrets to pull image, container level: mounts & env variables, resource allocation.
  • A template for a CronJob based deployment can look like this:
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: <job_name>
  namespace: <kyma_namespace>
spec:
  suspend: false
  schedule: "* * * * *" 
  timeZone: "Europe/Berlin"

  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      backoffLimit: 3
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: Never
          imagePullSecrets:
            - name: ghcr-pull-secret

          volumes:
            - name: gnupg-home
              emptyDir: {}

          initContainers:
            - name: init-gnupg
              image: busybox:1.36
              command: ["sh","-c","mkdir -p /tmp/gnupg && chmod 700 /tmp/gnupg"]
              volumeMounts:
                - name: gnupg-home
                  mountPath: /tmp/gnupg

              resources:
                requests:
                  cpu: "10m"
                  memory: "32Mi"
                limits:
                  cpu: "50m"
                  memory: "64Mi"

          containers:
            - name: inbound
              image: <container_registry_path>
              imagePullPolicy: Always
              command: [<custom_os_level_command_if_required>]
              volumeMounts:
                - name: gnupg-home
                  mountPath: /tmp/gnupg

              env:
                - name: KEY_PATH
                  value: "/keys/keyfile.key"
                - name: PASSPHRASE
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: kyma-secret
                      key: PASSPHRASE

              resources:
                requests:
                  cpu: "100m"
                  memory: "1Gi"
                limits:
                  cpu: "200m"
                  memory: "2Gi"

8 Achievements

​The following functionality and service details were delivered:

  • The Python code was improved multiple times: Handling approx. 30 files in one go with a total of 10 GB in encrypted state could be accelerated to < 30 minutes of processing time
  • Cron Jobs in the Kyma cluster can be used to schedule individual services, e.g. inbound processing of files that reside in a specific folder on HDLFS
  • SAP Datasphere writes local tables via Replication Flows back into HDLFS, on which the outbound service performs encryption and marks the files to „collectible“

9 More Scope

...up to come soon. I will cover aspects how to further mature the microservice/python logic and moreover exemplify on HTTP based encryption/decryption service endpoints, exposed on the Kyma Cluster.

10 References

​K8S/Kyma + Python SCN Blog

​Getting Started with Data Lake Files HDLFSCLI

​HDLFS REST API Guide

Thx...

...to my fellow colleagues at SAP who provides meaningful input and discussed about solution options. Furthermore I am especially grateful to our implementation partner who also supported, implemented and showed strong endurance in building up this scenario.

I hope you enjoyed reading this article and could have gained some deeper insights into what we did. If you have comments or suggestions of any kind, don't hesitate to comment and start-off a discussions with me and hopefully other SMEs.

entry.title: Re: JMS Mastery Series Part 1 - The Async Awakening
entry.content:
What should be the approach at the receiver side?

For example if the receiver an S4 system and the integration is only for point-to-point data replication.   Should the synchronous Odata API be used or it would be better to utilize SOAP API for true end-to-end async communication.
In other words where should the application errors be handled?  I assume in case of SOAP API, the application error does not go back to Integration Center, whereas in Odata it does.

Thanks.

entry.title: Re: Export all BOMs
entry.content: If you need a ready‑made solution, explore the BOM Excel Export micro‑app on SAP BTP. If you want a custom but flexible solution, build a CDS view for BOM header + item data and expose it for Excel download.

I think there you should be able to define null also

entry.title: Re: GBB-VBR Account Determination by Account Assignment C...
entry.content:
Recommended approach would be

Do not try to create Z modification keys (not possible in Public Cloud). Instead, use BAdI for account determination to implement conditional logic:

  • If account assignment = Cost Center → post to G/L A
  • If account assignment = WBS → post to G/L B
  • If account assignment = Order → post to G/L C

This way, you achieve the same outcome as having ZVBR, but within the supported extensibility framework.

entry.title: Re: Export all BOMs
entry.content: That looks interesting. We have BTP, but we've not done much with it. We are not sure if there is an additional cost for using it. There shouldn't be an additional cost for a basic BOM report like what we are needing. We'll look into this. Thanks!
